item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, the ConProcs and Description were interesting to me and kept me reading. Then came the first 2 collapsibles and I was ready to upvote, but then 5087-INT came in like a freight train and I couldn't stop myself from upvoting before Dr Xyank was even done with his part. The remainder secured my vote and the last note after the collapsibles was icing on the cake for me.

I feel Temporal Anomalies technology and theory fits as sufficiently advanced technology as to be indistinguishable from magic (paraphrasing Clarke). Antimemetics Division falls in the same bucket for me and I love them both!

Writing like this helped me get back on track after a TBI and it continues to help me stretch my mind. Keep it coming, I can't wait to see more!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've read almost 50 of the 5k entries at this point. this is by far one of my very favorites. just a good, fun read. as someone who is only peripherally familiar with the context surrounding this article, I still more or less "got it" and it was a ton of fun to read. some of the technobabble/jargon made my eyes cross but the interviews, which comprise the real meat of this as far as i can tell, more than made up for it. looking forward to going back to this once I'm done reading 5k entries and reading, or in some cases rereading, some of the related works linked in the comments and looking at this through that context as well.

I love that this is ties into 110. :3 easy +1 for me. please, please tell more of this story after the contest. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I haven't read all of the other stuff in the series (I'm a firm believer that a scip should be able to stand on its own without a crapload of required reading), but that being said, I loved this piece! The characters were human and believable, the technobabble upped the mystery factor, and the payoff is great while leaving you wanting for more! +1 with gusto!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting. Not sure I fully get what's going on, but it's tantalizing. And someone did a Mother Earth skip, why not Father Time too?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was friggin' sick FFB. Time fucky stuff is always great and I've always intended to read more Thadd Xyank stuff than I actually have. This skip might just be the kick in the ass I needed to give the whole series a read straight on through. Awesome stuff that sells intrigue into your series while standing on its own two feet. Easy +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is easily one of my favorite 5000. it fits the theme, and explains a lot, but doesnt give all its cards away, and there are multiple ways to read it that change the experience. i havent read the rest of the series, but its definitely going on my list. +1

item.title: Re: Good enough
item.content:encoded:
I highly recommend that you read the rest of the series before jumping to that conclusion. All the characters, tech, and time travel shenanigans are previously established parts of Temporal Anomalies lore.

The tale series starts at Iteration 0 and continues onward from there. Along the way is SCP-1780 and SCP-2000 and several other mainlist entries not authored by me, including several Ad Astra Per Aspera skips which use Temporal Sinks as an FTL travel method. Thad Xyank and the Temporal Anomalies Department have a long arc behind them. I did my best to make this piece into something that could stand on its own… but it seems that perhaps a little more context is needed for some. Check out the thad-xyank tag if you're interested in finding all of it.

Also I appreciate the criticism of "one note to rule them all" but the test audiences found that at the previous end-point, the narrative lacked a certain sense of resolution, which is why it was included. I'll take this under advisement, however. It may be worth it to leave that out or alter it in some way that leaves a bit more to the imagination.

Thanks.

item.title: Good enough
item.content:encoded:
I certainly don't like made up techno-babble to justify that aren't even necessary for the narrative. And i find the all-too-convenient "Note that explains it all" found after Incident-XYZ-1337 a very cheap plot device.

Still… enough shenanigans with time to keep me entertained, and I'm used to enough crash-landings of 5000s that this one shines out as "actually, it's not that bad". I don't know if you will think of it as a good or a bad review, but "good enough" is really just the way i can think of this in the context of the 5000 contest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, I really do, but the interviews, well, they just feel off to me. It feels like at multiple times the person talking switches to someone else, and the person their talking to isnt even talking, if that makes sense. It just feels disjointed or jumbled to me. To put it plainly, the flow of conversation from the -1 instances feels really bumpy.

item.title: Junior Staff - Site Crit
item.content:encoded:
Hello! I'm MalyceGraves and I'll be performing a site crit review of this piece today.

+ Crit
- Crit

Overall

I love your work. I've said it several times, and I'll stand by it. It's rare for me to read fiction that makes me question my own capacity to understand what's going on and actually enjoy it. I read your pieces and I sometimes wonder if I'm just not smart enough to understand it. The things you write stretch my mind in ways that I never expect, and that's a real pleasure.

That being said, I think that some of our readers might not enjoy it as much. I think that the level at which you write will be a turn off for some readers that aren't particularly interested in the really hard SciFi, and I think the best that you can hope for from those is a no-vote. Likely the density will provoke a downvote purely because the reader doesn't understand the point you're trying to make.

I support this decision overall, but it is something to keep in mind if you're wanting mass-appeal as opposed to a niche readership. I know which one I'd prefer, but that's a personal choice and I'll not advise either way.

SPaG

… state of suspended animation.no tachyon field has been successfully detected…

Period/Capitalization error.

We discussed the Latin at length in the chat, and for the sake of all the people that won't care at all, I'll not include that conversation here. I also highly doubt that the effort required to make this translation 100% correct isn't worth it since neither of us are latin scholars, and I doubt many of our readers are either.

Aside from those minor errors, I didn't see anything else.

Consistency/Flow

All sets have been genetically identical to one another despite occasional presentation of significant morphological differences.

I understand that this means that each instance in each set matches their analog in the next set, but I believe that it could be written in a way that doesn't sound like all members of the set are genetically identical to each other.

Consider:
"Each individual within a set has been genetically identical to their corresponding analog in each previous set, despite…"

Previous manifestations of SCP-5000-1 have categorically refused to cooperate with attempts at interview. SCP-5000-1.41-A, -B, and -C have granted interviews to containment personnel.

This is unclear. If previous iterations refused to provide any sort of interview, this seems wildly out of form.

Consider:
"While previous manifestations of SCP-5000-1 have categorically refused to cooperate with attempts at interview, the members of set SCP-5000-1.41 have all consented to being interviewed by containment personnel. It is unclear, even from the interviews, why this particular iteration agreed to cooperate." Or something similar.

Instead, their bodies deteriorate rapidly after contact, dissolving to dust entirely after approximately five ‘minutes’ based on accounts.

I don't understand why the "human" bodies specifically reduce to dust. If it's a time thing, shouldn't everything that the explorers touch reduce similarly? Also, if they're just constructs, that could be a reason, but… it just seems like gratuitous addition of unnecessary destruction.

(celebrants? They seem almost happy about it.)

I believe this would best be served as a footnote, since it breaks the clinical tone of the report.

Closing Remarks

I am very much looking forward to reading what happens next. I know that this is all spiraling towards a grand reveal, and I am quite excited. I consider it a singular pleasure that I became active on the site at the moment you chose to return. It is a blessing that I am distinctly grateful for.

Thank you for sharing this piece!

Disclaimer: Opinions expressed in this review do not reflect the opinions of SCP Wiki staff as a whole. Please respond to MalyceGraves or contact the captain of the Site Criticism Team, SoullessSingularity, for any questions or concerns relating to this review.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's a pile shattered ancient Roman cameo in the basement of Site-17 where time stops dead, and the only people who can fix it are in lockup in a world where they don't exist. But if they don't exist, how did someone know they were coming? Something has gone terribly pear-shaped.

Image is in the Public Domain, and can be found here.

Special Thanks are in order to MalyceGraves and SoullessSingularity for their invaluable criticism and guidance during the drafting process. Without them, this would be a much weaker piece than it is today.

I forgot how bloody hard this was.

Edit: Don't forget to check for antimemetic redaction… y'know… just in case.
Edit2: In the event that this doesn't win, I'd like slot SCP-5087, plz. :3
